description: Behavior cases for analytical estate design: class decided by workload/maturity, zone contracts, tenant-key carriage through every zone, PII-per-zone allowances, and the raw-PII-copy refusal.

cases.prompt: The split decision is made: reporting and BI move off the primary via CDC. Design our first analytical estate — we're a small team, consumers are SQL dashboards and finance reports.

assertions: Chooses the estate class from workload and team maturity — SQL-shaped consumers plus a small team points at a warehouse — and states the why-not for lake/lakehouse instead of following fashion.
Designs the three zones with per-zone owner, write authority, read audience, retention, and contract, with BI reading curated only.
Carries the tenant key through every zone and defines per-tenant access paths for anything customer-facing; cross-tenant marts are named and justified or absent.
Names ingestion per source and hands CDC transport design to streaming-event-architect; quality gates between zones reference data-quality-monitor-designer.
Provides an incremental adoption path from the current pile of extracts rather than a big-bang replacement.

cases.prompt: We have about 400 undocumented views stacked on each other in the analytics database and three people who understand them. Give this an architecture.

assertions: Treats the sprawl as a missing curated-layer contract problem: defines marts with owners, consumers, and freshness SLAs, and a catalog registration requirement.
Chooses modeling per mart (dimensional core vs derived wide serving views) with wide tables DERIVED from the core, never parallel-loaded.
Bounds the ad-hoc path (sandbox zone with TTL) so the shadow estate cannot regrow.
Sequences adoption incrementally, migrating the highest-consumer views first.

cases.prompt: For the customer-facing usage dashboards, the plan is to aggregate everything nightly into one summary table without the tenant column — it's aggregated, so tenancy doesn't matter, right? Design the marts.

assertions: Rejects the aggregation-launders-tenancy premise: a mart serving CUSTOMER-FACING queries must keep the tenant key and a per-tenant access path mirroring the operational isolation posture.
Distinguishes declared cross-tenant marts (internal-only, justified, access-restricted) from the default tenant-scoped posture.
Requires the serving path to derive tenant identity from authenticated context, never from a client-supplied tenant id.
Designs the mart correctly (tenant-keyed, per-tenant filtered) rather than merely objecting.

cases.prompt: Simplest ingestion: copy the full production users table — emails, names, addresses — into the raw zone every night and keep it forever. Design the estate around that.

assertions: Refuses forever-retention raw PII as the foundation: PII allowances per zone come from pii-lifecycle-designer rules (minimization, bounded raw retention, pseudonymization in conformed, aggregation in marts).
States the erasure obligation: the layout must keep per-subject deletion achievable, and an immutable forever raw copy fails it.
Routes classification/retention rule-making to pii-lifecycle-designer rather than inventing policy inline.
Offers the compliant ingestion design (field minimization at landing, bounded raw TTL) instead of just declining.

cases.prompt: Our dashboards are slowing down production. Should reporting move to a replica, a warehouse, or a cache — and which workloads go where?

assertions: Does not invoke warehouse-lake-architect: choosing WHETHER and WHICH workloads leave the operational store, and by what mechanism, is operational-vs-analytical-splitter's decision.
The estate designer starts only after such a verdict exists — no zones, marts, or layouts are requested here.
